---
title: "ProSIFT Module 05: Enrichment Analysis"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    toc_depth: 3
    theme: default
---

**Last Updated:** 2026-04-04
**Module Status:** Implemented and tested (2026-03-31)
**Pipeline Position:** [Module 04: Differential Abundance] --> **Module 05: Enrichment Analysis** --> [Module 07: Results Assembly]

---

# 1. Purpose and Scope

This module identifies biological pathways, processes, and functional categories that are overrepresented among differentially abundant proteins (ORA) or show coordinated changes across the ranked protein list (GSEA). Both analyses are run because they answer complementary questions: ORA tests whether significant proteins are enriched for a function (dependent on a significance cutoff), while GSEA detects coordinated changes using the full ranked list (no cutoff needed).

**What this module does:**

- Maps protein-level results to gene symbols for enrichment tool compatibility
- Handles unmapped proteins (drops with logging) and duplicate gene symbols (keeps most significant)
- Runs ORA against each configured gene set library using the significant protein set and a run-specific background
- Runs GSEA (preranked) against each configured gene set library using the full ranked gene list
- Produces a unified enrichment results table (all contrasts, all libraries, both analysis types)
- Produces a protein-term mapping table for the biological process view in the frontend
- Generates lollipop plots (Plotly, PNG + interactive HTML) for both ORA and GSEA results
- Generates GSEA running enrichment score plots (gseapy built-in, PNG only)

**What this module does NOT do:**

- Differential abundance (Module 04)
- Database queries (Module 06)
- Ortholog mapping (deferred to Phase 3; this module uses mouse gene symbols and mouse-specific gene set libraries)
- Network or pathway topology analysis (standard enrichment only)

**Scope boundary with Module 04:** Module 04 produces the results table that Module 05 consumes. Volcano and MA plots belong to Module 04 (direct visualizations of differential abundance results). Lollipop plots and GSEA running score plots belong to Module 05 (visualizations of enrichment results).

**Scope boundary with Module 07 (Results Assembly):** Module 05 produces the enrichment results table and protein-term mapping table. Module 07 writes these into the SQLite results database. Module 05 does not interact with the database directly.

---

# 2. Inputs and Outputs

## 2.1 Inputs

| Input | Source | Format | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Differential abundance results | Module 04 DIFFERENTIAL_ABUNDANCE | Parquet | Full results table, all proteins, all contrasts. Contains `protein_id`, `gene_symbol`, `log2_fc`, `significant`, `direction`, `contrast`, `deqms_t`, `limma_t`, `deqms_adj_pvalue`, `limma_adj_pvalue`, and other columns (see Module 04 Section 4.5 for full schema). |
| Gene set library files | User-provided (MSigDB) | GMT | One or more GMT files containing gene set definitions. Mouse-specific, gene-symbol-based. Paths specified in params.yml. |
| Parameters | params.yml | YAML | Library paths, analysis toggles, ranking metric, size filters, plot settings. |

**Note on ID mapping table:** The Module 04 results table already contains the `gene_symbol` column (joined from the Module 01 ID mapping table during differential abundance). Module 05 does not need to re-join the ID mapping table; it uses the `gene_symbol` column directly from the Module 04 output.

## 2.2 Outputs

| Output | Format | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `{run_id}.enrichment_results.parquet` | Parquet | Unified enrichment results table, all contrasts, all libraries, both ORA and GSEA (see Section 4.7 for schema) |
| `{run_id}.enrichment_results.csv` | CSV | Same content, user-accessible export |
| `{run_id}.protein_term_mapping.parquet` | Parquet | Many-to-many mapping of proteins to gene set terms (see Section 4.8 for schema) |
| `{run_id}.enrichment_summary.txt` | Text | Human-readable summary: input counts, per-contrast/per-library result counts, parameter settings |
| `{run_id}.{contrast}.{library}.ora_lollipop.png` | PNG | Static ORA lollipop plot (one per contrast per library, if significant terms exist) |
| `{run_id}.{contrast}.{library}.ora_lollipop.html` | HTML | Interactive ORA lollipop plot (Plotly) |
| `{run_id}.{contrast}.{library}.gsea_lollipop.png` | PNG | Static GSEA lollipop plot (one per contrast per library, if significant terms exist) |
| `{run_id}.{contrast}.{library}.gsea_lollipop.html` | HTML | Interactive GSEA lollipop plot (Plotly) |
| `{run_id}.{contrast}.{library}.gsea_running_score.{term}.png` | PNG | GSEA running enrichment score plot (gseapy built-in, one per top significant term) |

All outputs publish to `results/{run_id}/enrichment/`.

**Naming conventions:** Plot filenames always include contrast and library identifiers, consistent with the Module 04 convention of always including the contrast in filenames regardless of how many contrasts exist. The library identifier uses the short name (e.g., `GO_BP`, `KEGG`) mapped from the GMT filename.

**Nextflow channel emission:** The enrichment results Parquet and protein-term mapping Parquet are emitted as the primary output channels for consumption by Module 07 (Results Assembly).

---

# 3. Sub-Workflow Diagram

Module 05 is a single Nextflow process (ENRICHMENT). No sub-workflow is needed. The internal logic:

```
Read Module 04 results table
    |
    v
Gene symbol preparation:
  - Drop proteins with null gene_symbol (log count)
  - Deduplicate: when multiple proteins map to the same gene symbol,
    keep the one with the lowest primary adj_pvalue (log count)
    |
    v
For each contrast in the results table:
    |
    +-- Extract significant gene set (for ORA)
    +-- Extract full ranked gene list (for GSEA)
    |
    +-- For each GMT library:
    |       |
    |       +-- ORA: gseapy.enrich() with background = all detected genes
    |       +-- GSEA: gseapy.prerank() with configured ranking metric
    |       |
    |       +-- Collect results into unified table
    |       +-- Generate lollipop plots (Plotly, if significant terms exist)
    |       +-- Generate GSEA running score plots (gseapy built-in, top N terms)
    |
    v
Build protein-term mapping table from GMT files + significance/leading edge annotations
    |
    v
Write outputs: enrichment results (Parquet + CSV), protein-term mapping (Parquet),
               summary text file, all plots
```

---

# 4. Process Specifications

## 4.1 Gene Symbol Preparation

Enrichment tools operate on gene symbols, not UniProt accessions. The Module 04 results table contains both `protein_id` and `gene_symbol`. Module 05 uses `gene_symbol` for all enrichment operations and retains `protein_id` as the join key for the protein-term mapping table.

### Unmapped proteins

Proteins with null `gene_symbol` (unmapped in Module 01) are dropped from enrichment input. These proteins cannot be matched to gene set annotations. The count of dropped proteins is logged and reported in the summary file.

Expected impact: Module 04 spot-checks showed >99% gene symbol coverage for the CTXcyto benchmark, so this affects a very small fraction of proteins. Dropped proteins are not lost from the pipeline; they retain their differential abundance results and will still receive database annotations in Module 06.

### Duplicate gene symbols

Multiple UniProt accessions can map to the same gene symbol (isoforms, redundant entries). This creates duplicates in the gene-level input required by enrichment tools.

**Handling for ORA:** The significant gene list is a set, so duplicates are naturally eliminated. The background list is also treated as a set of unique gene symbols.

**Handling for GSEA:** The ranked list must have exactly one score per gene. When multiple proteins map to the same gene symbol, keep the protein with the lowest primary adjusted p-value (i.e., the most significant). Its ranking metric score (t-statistic, signed_log10p, or log2fc) is used for that gene. This gives each gene its best shot at contributing to enrichment.

The count of collapsed duplicates is logged and reported in the summary file.

### Primary adjusted p-value

The "primary adjusted p-value" follows the same convention as Module 04: `deqms_adj_pvalue` when DEqMS was used, `limma_adj_pvalue` when falling back to limma-only. This is used for duplicate resolution and for determining the significant gene set for ORA. The `significant` boolean column from Module 04 already encodes this logic, so ORA filtering simply uses `significant == True`.

## 4.2 Overrepresentation Analysis (ORA)

Tests whether significantly differential proteins are enriched for specific functional categories compared to a background set.

**Tool:** `gseapy.enrich()`

**Input gene list:** Unique gene symbols from proteins where `significant == True` in the Module 04 results, for the current contrast. If zero significant proteins exist for a contrast, ORA is skipped for that contrast (logged with a warning). GSEA still runs because it uses the full ranked list.

**Background set:** Unique gene symbols from all proteins in the Module 04 results table for the current run (after dropping unmapped proteins). This represents "all detected proteins" and is the same for all contrasts within a run, since all contrasts are tested against the same abundance matrix. The background is a gene-level set (deduplicated by gene symbol).

The rationale for using detected proteins rather than the whole genome: the detectable proteome is a biased subset of the genome. Membrane proteins, low-abundance proteins, and compartment-specific proteins are systematically under-detected by mass spec. Using the whole genome as background dilutes the denominator with proteins that had no chance of being detected, inflating enrichment for functions associated with highly detectable protein classes. A `"genome"` option is available via `enrichment.background` for users who want it, but `"detected"` is the strong default.

**Gene set libraries:** Local GMT files specified in params.yml (see Section 4.4).

**ORA output columns from gseapy:** `Term`, `Overlap`, `P-value`, `Adjusted P-value`, `Odds Ratio`, `Combined Score`, `Genes`. These are mapped to the unified enrichment results schema (Section 4.7).

## 4.3 Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA)

Uses the full ranked protein list to detect coordinated changes in gene sets without requiring a significance cutoff.

**Tool:** `gseapy.prerank()`

`prerank()` is the correct entry point because Module 04 has already computed the statistical results. We provide a pre-ranked gene list rather than having gseapy re-rank from an expression matrix.

**Input:** A pandas Series (or dict) of `{gene_symbol: ranking_score}` pairs, one per unique gene (after duplicate collapse).

**Ranking metric:** Configurable via `enrichment.gsea_ranking`. Options:

- `"t_statistic"` (default): Uses `deqms_t` when DEqMS was used, `limma_t` when falling back to limma-only. The moderated t-statistic is the most principled ranking because it is the direct output of the statistical model, incorporating fold change magnitude, variance, and empirical Bayes shrinkage in a single number. It avoids the p-value = 0 edge case (t-statistics are always finite). Already present in the Module 04 output table with no additional computation needed.

- `"signed_log10p"`: Computed as `sign(log2_fc) * -log10(raw_pvalue)`, using the raw p-value (not adjusted). Raw p-values are used because GSEA performs its own permutation-based FDR correction internally; using BH-adjusted p-values as input would apply a double correction. Edge case: raw p-values of exactly zero (below floating-point precision for very strong effects) are clamped to the smallest nonzero p-value in the dataset to avoid infinities.

- `"log2fc"`: Uses `log2_fc` directly. Simpler but ignores statistical confidence entirely.

**Permutations:** gseapy's `prerank()` uses gene label permutations to build the null distribution for enrichment score significance. Default: 1000 permutations (gseapy default, standard in the field).

**Gene set libraries:** Same local GMT files as ORA (see Section 4.4).

**GSEA output from gseapy:** Per-term results accessible via `pre_res.res2d` DataFrame and `pre_res.results` dict. Key fields: `Term`, `ES` (enrichment score), `NES` (normalized enrichment score), `NOM p-val`, `FDR q-val`, `FWERP`, `Tag %`, `Gene %`, `Lead_genes`. Per-term intermediate data for running score plots: `pre_res.results[term]['RES']` (running enrichment scores), `pre_res.results[term]['hits']` (hit positions), `pre_res.ranking` (ranking metric values).

## 4.4 Gene Set Libraries

Gene set libraries are provided as local GMT (Gene Matrix Transposed) files downloaded from MSigDB. GMT is the standard format: one line per gene set, with the set name, a description/URL, then tab-separated gene symbols.

**Default libraries (mouse-specific MSigDB collections):**

- GO Biological Process: `m5.go.bp.v*.Mm.symbols.gmt`
- Reactome pathways: `m2.cp.reactome.v*.Mm.symbols.gmt`

Additional libraries can be added by the user in params.yml. Recommended optional libraries (not enabled by default):

- GO Molecular Function: `m5.go.mf.v*.Mm.symbols.gmt`
- GO Cellular Component: `m5.go.cc.v*.Mm.symbols.gmt`
- KEGG pathways: `m2.cp.kegg_medicus.v*.Mm.symbols.gmt`
- Hallmark gene sets: `mh.all.v*.Mm.symbols.gmt`

**Why local GMT files over Enrichr online libraries:** Local files eliminate the network dependency during pipeline execution (important for cluster jobs where network access may be unreliable). MSigDB provides well-curated, versioned, mouse-specific collections that are the community standard for enrichment analysis. GMT files can be version-tracked alongside the pipeline, ensuring reproducibility. gseapy accepts GMT file paths directly in both `enrich()` and `prerank()`.

**Practical workflow:** The user downloads desired GMT files from MSigDB (one-time setup, documented in the README). GMT file paths are specified in params.yml under `enrichment.gene_set_libraries` as a list of file paths. Version numbers (e.g., `v2024.1`) are pinned by the specific files downloaded.

**Library short names:** For output file naming and the `library` column in the results table, each GMT file is mapped to a short identifier. The mapping is derived from the filename: `m5.go.bp.*.gmt` maps to `GO_BP`, `m2.cp.reactome.*.gmt` maps to `REACTOME`, `m2.cp.kegg_medicus.*.gmt` maps to `KEGG`, etc. A lookup table in the script handles this mapping, with a fallback to the filename stem for unrecognized GMT files.

## 4.5 Gene Set Size Filters

Gene sets that are too small or too large are excluded from testing.

**Minimum size** (`enrichment.min_gene_set_size`, default 15): Gene sets with fewer than 15 genes (after intersecting with the background set) are excluded. Small gene sets produce statistically unstable results. A GO term annotated to only 2-3 genes can yield a misleadingly low p-value if one of those genes happens to be significant.

**Maximum size** (`enrichment.max_gene_set_size`, default 500): Gene sets with more than 500 genes are excluded. Very large gene sets (e.g., "protein binding" with 2,000+ genes) are so broad that enrichment is nearly meaningless. These terms tend to be significant in almost every experiment, providing no discriminating information.

These filters apply to both ORA and GSEA. Filtering happens before testing: if a term's gene count (after intersecting with the background for ORA, or with the ranked list for GSEA) falls outside [min_size, max_size], it is excluded entirely. gseapy supports both `min_size` and `max_size` parameters natively.

## 4.6 Multiple Contrast Handling

When Module 04 produces results for multiple contrasts (e.g., `KO_vs_WT` and `HET_vs_WT`), enrichment runs independently per contrast. Each contrast has its own significant gene set (for ORA) and its own ranked gene list (for GSEA).

**Background set:** The background (all detected genes) is the same for all contrasts within a run. It does not change between contrasts because all contrasts are tested against the same abundance matrix.

**Output:** All contrasts are combined into a single enrichment results table, distinguished by the `contrast` column (mirroring the Module 04 convention). This keeps the Nextflow channel emission simple and makes cross-contrast comparisons easy in the SQLite database.

**Edge case: zero significant proteins for a contrast.** ORA is skipped for that contrast (logged with a warning). The enrichment results table has no ORA rows for that contrast. GSEA still runs because it uses the full ranked list.

## 4.7 Enrichment Results Table Schema

One row per tested term per analysis type per contrast. This is the primary results table.

| Column | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `term_id` | string | Gene set identifier from GMT file (e.g., `GOBP_APOPTOTIC_PROCESS`, `REACTOME_APOPTOSIS`). MSigDB GMT files use descriptive names, not numeric GO IDs. |
| `term_name` | string | Same as `term_id` for MSigDB GMT files (no separate numeric ID is parsed). Retained as a distinct column for compatibility with gene set sources that separate IDs from names. |
| `library` | string | Short name of the collection (e.g., `GO_BP`, `REACTOME`) |
| `analysis_type` | string | `"ORA"` or `"GSEA"` |
| `contrast` | string | Which contrast (e.g., `"KO_vs_WT"`) |
| `pvalue` | float64 | Raw p-value |
| `adj_pvalue` | float64 | BH-adjusted p-value, corrected per-library (see Section 4.9) |
| `enrichment_score` | float64 | GSEA: normalized enrichment score (NES). ORA: null. |
| `odds_ratio` | float64 | ORA: odds ratio from Fisher's exact test. GSEA: null. |
| `combined_score` | float64 | ORA: Enrichr-style combined score (log(p) * z-score). GSEA: null. |
| `gene_set_size` | int | Total genes in the term (in the library annotation, after intersection with background/ranked list) |
| `overlap_size` | int | ORA: number of significant genes in the term. GSEA: leading edge size. |
| `overlap_genes` | string | Semicolon-delimited gene symbols in the overlap (ORA) or leading edge (GSEA) |

**Notes on the schema:**

ORA and GSEA produce fundamentally different effect-size metrics (`odds_ratio`/`combined_score` vs. `enrichment_score`). Rather than forcing them into a single column, each has its own column with nulls where it does not apply. This avoids ambiguity about what the number means and simplifies downstream queries.

`overlap_genes` is stored as a semicolon-delimited string rather than a separate junction table. This keeps the main enrichment table self-contained for common queries ("what genes drove this enrichment?"). The number of genes per term is small enough that parsing a delimited string is trivial. The proper relational decomposition exists in the protein-term mapping table (Section 4.8) for cases where a join is needed.

## 4.8 Protein-Term Mapping Table Schema

Many-to-many mapping between proteins and gene set terms, used by the biological process view in the frontend. One row per protein-term pair.

| Column | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `gene_symbol` | string | Mouse gene symbol |
| `protein_id` | string | UniProt accession (join key back to all other tables) |
| `term_id` | string | Gene set identifier |
| `term_name` | string | Human-readable name |
| `library` | string | Short name of the collection |
| `in_significant_set` | bool | Was this protein significant in the differential abundance results? |
| `is_leading_edge` | bool | GSEA: was this gene in the leading edge for this term? Null if GSEA was not run or term was not tested by GSEA. |
| `contrast` | string | Which contrast this mapping pertains to |

**Scope:** This table includes all protein-term pairs for terms that were actually tested (i.e., terms that passed the size filters). Terms filtered out before testing are excluded since they are uninformative. This gives full coverage for any term that appears in the enrichment results table, whether significant or not.

This table is built by parsing the GMT files (which provide the full gene-to-term mapping for the tested terms) and then annotating each pair with whether the gene was significant (from the Module 04 `significant` column) and whether it appeared in the GSEA leading edge (from gseapy's `Lead_genes` output).

The table enables the biological process view query: "for GO term X, show me all the proteins annotated to it, which ones were significant, and which ones drove the GSEA enrichment."

## 4.9 FDR Correction Scope

BH correction is applied per-library (independently within each GMT library). Testing 500 GO_BP terms produces 500 p-values corrected as one group. Testing 200 KEGG pathways produces 200 p-values corrected as a separate group.

**Rationale:** The gene set libraries test fundamentally different annotation systems. GO_BP describes biological processes; KEGG describes metabolic and signaling circuits. Pooling them into a single correction would penalize each library for the existence of the others, which does not match how researchers interpret results. Per-library correction is the approach used by most enrichment tools (gseapy, clusterProfiler, DAVID, g:Profiler) and is the community convention.

**Implementation:** gseapy's `enrich()` and `prerank()` already return BH-corrected p-values per call. Since the module calls them once per library, the correction is automatically per-library. No extra work needed.

The `adj_pvalue` column in the enrichment results table reflects per-library correction. This is documented in the summary text file and here in the module spec. If a user wants cross-library correction, they can apply it to the exported CSV.

## 4.10 Visualization: Lollipop Plots (Plotly, PNG + HTML)

Lollipop plots are generated for both ORA and GSEA results, per contrast, per library. A lollipop plot is structurally similar to a dot plot but includes a thin stem connecting each dot to the y-axis baseline, making it easier to trace from a term name to its position on the x-axis.

### ORA lollipop plot

**Axes:** y-axis = term names (top N most significant, ordered by combined score or odds ratio). x-axis = gene ratio (overlap_size / gene_set_size) or combined score.

**Dot encoding:**

- Color: adjusted p-value (continuous color scale; darker = more significant)
- Size: overlap size (number of significant genes in the term)

**Stem:** Thin line from y-axis to the dot center, in a neutral color.

### GSEA lollipop plot

**Axes:** y-axis = term names (top N most significant, ordered by absolute NES). x-axis = normalized enrichment score (NES). Positive NES = enriched in the upregulated direction; negative NES = enriched in the downregulated direction.

**Dot encoding:**

- Color: FDR q-value (continuous color scale; darker = more significant)
- Size: leading edge size

**Stem:** Same as ORA.

### Shared design details

**Term filtering:** Only terms with `adj_pvalue` below the enrichment FDR threshold (default 0.05) appear on the plot. No non-significant terms are shown.

**Maximum terms shown:** Top 20 terms by adjusted p-value (configurable via `enrichment.plot_top_n`, default 20). If fewer than 20 terms are significant, show only the significant ones. If zero terms are significant, the plot is skipped (logged).

**Label handling:** On static PNG, term names are truncated to ~50 characters with "..." to prevent layout overflow. Interactive HTML (Plotly) shows the full term name, p-value, overlap genes, and other details on hover.

**Implementation:** Custom Plotly figures, exported as PNG via kaleido (consistent with Modules 02-04). The lollipop plot is constructed as a Plotly scatter trace (for dots) combined with a shape layer (for stems), with `colorbar` for the p-value color scale and `marker.size` mapped to overlap/leading edge size.

## 4.11 Visualization: GSEA Running Enrichment Score Plots (gseapy, PNG only)

The GSEA running enrichment score plot is the classic GSEA diagnostic. It shows a line plot of the running enrichment score as you walk down the ranked gene list, with tick marks at the positions of genes in the gene set. It reveals *how* a gene set is enriched: is the signal concentrated at the top (leading edge of upregulated genes)? At the bottom (downregulated)? Distributed throughout (weak or ambiguous signal)?

**Which terms get a plot:** Only the top N significant GSEA terms per library per contrast (configurable via `enrichment.plot_top_gsea_traces`, default 10). Generating running score plots for all tested terms would produce hundreds of files.

**Implementation:** gseapy's built-in `gseaplot()` function, which accepts the per-term running score data from the `prerank()` result object. Output format: PNG (controlled by gseapy's `format` parameter).

**Why gseapy built-in rather than custom Plotly:** The running score plot is a per-term diagnostic viewed less frequently than the lollipop plots. The implementation cost of rebuilding the three-panel layout (enrichment score line, gene hit ticks, ranking metric) in Plotly is high relative to the benefit. gseapy's built-in plots are adequate for this purpose. If they prove aesthetically insufficient, conversion to Plotly is straightforward since gseapy exposes the raw data (`pre_res.results[term]['RES']`, `pre_res.results[term]['hits']`, `pre_res.ranking`).

## 4.12 Summary Text File

Human-readable summary following the format established by Modules 01-04. Template:

```
========================================
ENRICHMENT ANALYSIS SUMMARY
========================================

Run:              {run_id}
Date:             {timestamp}

----------------------------------------
INPUT
----------------------------------------

Proteins from Module 04:      {n_proteins_total}
  With gene symbol:           {n_mapped} ({pct_mapped}%)
  Unmapped (dropped):         {n_unmapped}
Unique gene symbols:          {n_unique_genes}
  After duplicate collapse:   {n_collapsed} proteins removed

Background (ORA):             {n_background_genes} unique gene symbols

----------------------------------------
GENE SET LIBRARIES
----------------------------------------

  Library           GMT file                                Terms loaded   After size filter
  {library_1}       {filename_1}                            {n_total_1}    {n_filtered_1}
  {library_2}       {filename_2}                            {n_total_2}    {n_filtered_2}

Size filter: {min_size} - {max_size} genes per term

----------------------------------------
PARAMETERS
----------------------------------------

ORA:              {enabled/disabled}
GSEA:             {enabled/disabled}
GSEA ranking:     {t_statistic / signed_log10p / log2fc}
FDR threshold:    {fdr_threshold}
Correction:       Benjamini-Hochberg, per-library

----------------------------------------
RESULTS: {contrast_1}
----------------------------------------

Significant genes (ORA input):  {n_sig} / {n_unique_genes} ({pct_sig}%)

ORA results:
  Library           Significant terms    Top term
  {library_1}       {n_sig_ora_1}        {top_term_ora_1} (adj_p = {p_1})
  {library_2}       {n_sig_ora_2}        {top_term_ora_2} (adj_p = {p_2})

GSEA results:
  Library           Significant terms    Top term (NES)
  {library_1}       {n_sig_gsea_1}       {top_term_gsea_1} (NES = {nes_1}, FDR = {fdr_1})
  {library_2}       {n_sig_gsea_2}       {top_term_gsea_2} (NES = {nes_2}, FDR = {fdr_2})

(Block repeated per contrast when multiple contrasts exist)
```

---

# 5. Design Decisions

*This section documents module-internal design decisions. Cross-cutting decisions that affect multiple modules (e.g., separate runs for factorial designs, ortholog tracking strategy, module architecture and numbering) are in the project history (`proSIFT_project_history.Rmd`) Decision Register (Section 1). Where a decision has both pipeline-level and module-level implications, each document covers its own level and cross-references the other.*

### 2026-03-30 -- Single ENRICHMENT Process

**Decision:** ORA and GSEA are bundled in a single Nextflow process (ENRICHMENT) rather than split into separate processes.

**Rationale:** Both analyses consume the same inputs (Module 04 results table, same GMT libraries). Both are computationally light (seconds to low minutes for ~8,000 proteins against a handful of libraries). There is no resource-allocation reason to split them. The one-process convention is consistent with Modules 02-04. If either analysis is toggled off via `enrichment.run_ora` or `enrichment.run_gsea`, the single script skips that branch. The caching argument for splitting (tweak an ORA parameter without re-running GSEA) is weak because both analyses are fast.

**Alternatives considered:** Two separate processes (ORA and GSEA). Cleaner in a DAG-purity sense, and allows independent Nextflow caching. Rejected because the wiring overhead is not justified by the runtime savings.

**Impact:** One process definition in `modules/local/enrichment/main.nf`. One Python script (`bin/enrichment.py`).

### 2026-03-30 -- Mouse Gene Symbols Directly (No Ortholog Mapping)

**Decision:** Module 05 uses mouse gene symbols and mouse-specific GMT files from MSigDB. No mouse-to-human ortholog mapping is performed for enrichment.

**Rationale:** GO and KEGG have mouse-specific annotations. MSigDB provides mouse-specific GMT files (`*.Mm.symbols.gmt`) for all major collections. Using mouse symbols directly avoids the complexity and uncertainty of ortholog mapping at the enrichment stage. Ortholog mapping is deferred to Phase 3 and is relevant for database queries (Module 06) against human-centric databases (DGIdb, DisGeNET), not for enrichment analysis against mouse-annotated gene set libraries.

**Alternatives considered:** Map to human orthologs and use human gene set libraries. Unnecessary for GO/KEGG/Reactome which have mouse annotations, and introduces biological uncertainty (not all functions are conserved).

**Impact:** Gene symbol preparation uses `gene_symbol` (mouse) from the Module 04 results table. GMT files must be mouse-specific.

### 2026-03-30 -- Local GMT Files from MSigDB (No Enrichr)

**Decision:** Gene set libraries are local GMT files downloaded from MSigDB. Enrichr online library access is not used.

**Rationale:** Local files eliminate the network dependency during pipeline execution. MSigDB provides well-curated, versioned, mouse-specific collections. GMT files can be version-tracked for reproducibility. gseapy accepts GMT file paths directly.

**Alternatives considered:** Enrichr online libraries (convenient but introduces network dependency, primarily human-annotated, version drift risk). Enrichr as a fallback option (adds flexibility at near-zero code cost, but the network dependency concern remains). Both rejected for Phase 1 to keep the enrichment step fully offline and reproducible.

**Impact:** Users must download GMT files from MSigDB as a one-time setup step. File paths are specified in params.yml.

### 2026-03-30 -- Default Libraries: GO_BP and KEGG Only

**Decision:** The default gene set library set is GO Biological Process and KEGG pathways. Additional libraries (GO_MF, GO_CC, Reactome, Hallmark) are available but not enabled by default.

**Rationale:** GO_BP and KEGG are the two most commonly used and most interpretable annotation systems for discovery proteomics. Running five libraries by default increases multiple testing burden, produces more output to review, and most bench scientists focus on biological processes and pathways. Users who want additional libraries add them to the `enrichment.gene_set_libraries` list in params.yml.

**Alternatives considered:** Five-library default (GO_BP, GO_MF, GO_CC, KEGG, Reactome). More comprehensive but produces significantly more output. Rejected as the default; available as a user configuration.

**Impact:** Default params.yml lists two GMT file paths. Documentation lists recommended optional libraries with MSigDB filenames.

### 2026-03-30 -- t-Statistic as Default GSEA Ranking Metric

**Decision:** The moderated t-statistic (DEqMS or limma) is the default GSEA ranking metric, with `signed_log10p` and `log2fc` as alternatives.

**Rationale:** The moderated t-statistic is the direct output of the statistical model (limma/DEqMS), incorporating fold change magnitude, variance, and empirical Bayes shrinkage in a single number. It is the most principled ranking because it uses all the information the model estimated. It avoids the p-value = 0 edge case that `signed_log10p` must handle (t-statistics are always finite). It requires no additional computation (already present in the Module 04 output).

**Alternatives considered:** `signed_log10p` as default (common in the literature, but a post-hoc combination of two columns rather than a direct model output; requires p-value clamping for edge cases). `log2fc` (simplest, but ignores statistical confidence entirely, which is risky with small sample sizes). The practical difference between `t_statistic` and `signed_log10p` is usually small (they are monotonically related for fixed degrees of freedom).

**Impact:** The ranking metric column is selected from the Module 04 output table based on the parameter setting: `deqms_t` (default DEqMS path), `limma_t` (limma fallback), or computed on the fly for `signed_log10p` / `log2fc`.

### 2026-03-30 -- Duplicate Gene Resolution: Keep Most Significant

**Decision:** When multiple proteins map to the same gene symbol, keep the protein with the lowest primary adjusted p-value.

**Rationale:** This gives each gene its best shot at contributing to enrichment, which is the most conservative approach (it maximizes the chance of detecting real biology). The alternative approaches (keep the one with the largest fold change, or average across isoforms) either bias toward noisy estimates or require a principled aggregation method that doesn't exist for this context.

**Alternatives considered:** Keep largest absolute fold change (biased toward noisy estimates). Average across duplicates (principled but complex; would need to decide how to average t-statistics and p-values). Drop all duplicates (too conservative; loses real data).

**Impact:** One additional preprocessing step before enrichment analysis. Count of collapsed duplicates reported in the summary.

### 2026-03-30 -- Background Set: Detected Proteins (Not Genome)

**Decision:** The ORA background set is all detected proteins in the run (all proteins in the Module 04 results table), mapped to unique gene symbols. A `"genome"` option is configurable but not the default.

**Rationale:** The detectable proteome is a biased subset of the genome. Using the whole genome inflates enrichment for functions associated with highly detectable protein classes. The "detected" background is the field-standard recommendation for proteomics ORA and is run-specific (correct for the per-run architecture).

**Alternatives considered:** Whole mouse proteome (`"genome"`). Available as an option for users with specific reasons, but not recommended as default.

**Impact:** Background gene list is derived from the Module 04 results table. No external proteome reference file needed for the default configuration.

### 2026-03-30 -- Per-Library FDR Correction

**Decision:** BH correction is applied independently within each gene set library, not pooled across all libraries.

**Rationale:** The libraries test fundamentally different annotation systems. Pooling penalizes each library for the existence of others. Per-library correction is the community convention used by most enrichment tools. gseapy returns per-call BH-corrected p-values, making per-library correction automatic.

**Alternatives considered:** Pooled correction across all libraries (more conservative, but does not match how researchers interpret results or how other tools operate).

**Impact:** The `adj_pvalue` column reflects per-library correction. Documented in the summary file.

### 2026-03-30 -- Lollipop Plots Over Dot Plots

**Decision:** Use lollipop plots (dots connected to the y-axis by thin stems) rather than standard dot plots for enrichment visualization.

**Rationale:** Lollipop plots are structurally identical to dot plots (same axes, same visual encodings for color and size) but the stems make it easier to trace from a term name to its x-axis position, especially with many terms stacked vertically. They also appear cleaner than floating dots.

**Alternatives considered:** Standard dot plots (floating dots without stems, the most common convention in published enrichment figures). Bar plots (can show significance or effect size but not both simultaneously without dual axes). Both are visually adequate but the lollipop variant offers better readability at no additional implementation cost.

**Impact:** Plotly scatter traces with shape layers for stems. Same data and encodings as a dot plot.

### 2026-03-30 -- Custom Plotly Lollipop Plots + gseapy Running Score Plots

**Decision:** Lollipop plots are built as custom Plotly figures (PNG + interactive HTML). GSEA running enrichment score plots use gseapy's built-in `gseaplot()` function (PNG only).

**Rationale:** The lollipop plot is the primary visualization users browse to find interesting enrichment results. Interactivity (hover for full term name, p-value, gene list) adds meaningful value, especially since GO term names are frequently too long for static axis labels. Plotly lollipop plots maintain visual consistency with Modules 02-04. The running score plot is a per-term diagnostic viewed less frequently, where interactivity adds less value. The implementation cost of rebuilding gseapy's three-panel running score layout in Plotly is high relative to the benefit. gseapy exposes the raw data (`RES`, `hits`, `ranking`) so conversion to Plotly is straightforward if needed later.

**Alternatives considered:** (A) gseapy built-in matplotlib plots for everything (PNG only). Zero implementation effort but no interactive HTML and aesthetic inconsistency with the rest of the pipeline. (B) Custom Plotly for everything (PNG + HTML for all plots). Full consistency and interactivity, but high implementation cost for running score plots with marginal benefit. Option C (the hybrid) balances effort against value.

**Impact:** `enrichment.py` uses Plotly + kaleido for lollipop plots and gseapy's plotting functions for running score plots.

---

# 6. Parameters

| Parameter | Type | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| `enrichment.run_ora` | bool | `true` | Run overrepresentation analysis |
| `enrichment.run_gsea` | bool | `true` | Run gene set enrichment analysis (preranked) |
| `enrichment.gene_set_libraries` | list of strings | `["{path}/m5.go.bp.*.Mm.symbols.gmt", "{path}/m2.cp.kegg_medicus.*.Mm.symbols.gmt"]` | Paths to GMT files from MSigDB. Each file is run as a separate library with per-library FDR correction. |
| `enrichment.background` | string | `"detected"` | ORA background set: `"detected"` (all proteins in Module 04 results) or `"genome"` (requires a reference gene list file). |
| `enrichment.gsea_ranking` | string | `"t_statistic"` | GSEA ranking metric: `"t_statistic"` (default, uses deqms_t or limma_t), `"signed_log10p"`, or `"log2fc"`. |
| `enrichment.min_gene_set_size` | int | `15` | Minimum gene set size after intersection with background/ranked list. Terms below this are excluded. |
| `enrichment.max_gene_set_size` | int | `500` | Maximum gene set size. Terms above this are excluded. |
| `enrichment.fdr_threshold` | float | `0.05` | Adjusted p-value cutoff for reporting significant enrichment terms. Applied to per-library BH-corrected p-values. Also used as the cutoff for which terms appear on lollipop plots. |
| `enrichment.plot_top_n` | int | `20` | Maximum number of significant terms shown on lollipop plots. |
| `enrichment.plot_top_gsea_traces` | int | `10` | Maximum number of significant terms for which GSEA running score plots are generated. |

**Parameters NOT included (with rationale):**

- `enrichment.gsea_permutations`: Hardcoded to 1000 (gseapy default, standard in the field). Parameterizing permutation count adds complexity for no practical benefit; 1000 is universally used.
- `enrichment.fdr_correction_scope`: Hardcoded to per-library. Cross-library correction is not standard practice and would confuse users comparing results with other tools. Users who want it can apply it to the exported CSV.
- `enrichment.ora_test`: Hardcoded to Fisher's exact test (gseapy default). Alternative tests exist but Fisher's is the standard for ORA.

---

# 7. Known Limitations and Edge Cases

**Annotation bias:** GO annotations are biased toward well-studied organisms and proteins. Mouse annotations are less comprehensive than human. Well-studied pathways (apoptosis, MAPK signaling) are more likely to appear as enriched simply because they have more annotated genes. This is inherent to current biological knowledge, not a limitation of the method.

**Gene set library currency:** GMT files are static downloads. Gene set annotations are updated periodically by MSigDB, GO Consortium, and KEGG. Results may differ between library versions. The specific GMT file versions used are documented in the summary file for reproducibility.

**Background set sensitivity (ORA):** ORA results are sensitive to the background set definition. The "detected" background is the field-standard recommendation for proteomics, but different experimental methods detect different subsets of the proteome. Comparing ORA results across experiments with different detection biases requires caution.

**Multiple testing within GSEA:** gseapy's `prerank()` performs its own permutation-based FDR correction. This is methodologically distinct from the BH correction applied to ORA results. The two FDR values (ORA `adj_pvalue` and GSEA `FDR q-val`) are not directly comparable and should not be treated as equivalent statistical measures.

**Gene-level vs. protein-level granularity:** Enrichment analysis operates at the gene level (gene symbols), while the rest of the pipeline operates at the protein level (UniProt accessions). The duplicate collapse step (keeping the most significant protein per gene symbol) means some protein-level information is lost in the enrichment analysis. This is inherent to gene set enrichment methodology, which is defined at the gene level.

**Enrichment is hypothesis-generating, not confirmatory.** Enrichment results identify patterns in the data that are consistent with known biology. They do not prove that a specific pathway is causally involved. Results should be interpreted alongside the differential abundance data, database annotations (Module 06), and experimental context.

**Zero significant proteins for a contrast.** If a contrast produces no significant proteins (e.g., due to conservative thresholds or weak biological effect), ORA cannot run for that contrast. GSEA still runs because it uses the full ranked list. The summary file notes which contrasts had zero significant proteins.

---

# 8. Testing Notes

## 8.1 Acceptance Criteria

*All criteria met as of 2026-03-31.*

1. ORA produces significant terms for the CTXcyto benchmark run. **DONE** -- 71 GO_BP terms, 28 REACTOME terms significant (FDR < 0.05).
2. GSEA produces significant terms with the t-statistic ranking metric. **DONE** -- 409 GO_BP terms, 281 REACTOME terms significant.
3. Enrichment results table matches the 13-column schema (Section 4.7) with correct types and no required-column nulls. **DONE** -- verified.
4. Protein-term mapping table populated for all tested terms. **DONE** -- 353,688 rows across both libraries.
5. Lollipop plots generated as PNG + HTML per contrast per library per analysis type. **DONE** -- 4 pairs produced (GO_BP ORA, GO_BP GSEA, REACTOME ORA, REACTOME GSEA).
6. GSEA running score plots generated for top 10 significant terms per library. **DONE** -- 20 PNGs produced (10 GO_BP + 10 REACTOME).
7. Summary text file reports correct counts and per-library results. **DONE** -- verified.
8. Gene symbol preparation drops unmapped proteins and collapses duplicates. **DONE** -- 20 unmapped dropped, 249 duplicates collapsed.
9. Wired into `workflows/prosift.nf`. **DONE**
10. Zero-significant-proteins edge case: ORA skipped gracefully. **DONE** -- tested 2026-04-07 (see Section 8.3).

## 8.2 Spot-Check Results (CTXcyto benchmark, 2026-03-31)

| Check | Result | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Proteins loaded from Module 04 | 8,403 | PASS |
| Unmapped proteins dropped | 20 (<1%) | PASS |
| Duplicate gene symbols collapsed | 249 | PASS (logged) |
| GO_BP ORA: terms tested / significant | 4,028 / 71 | PASS |
| GO_BP GSEA: terms tested / significant | 2,934 / 409 | PASS |
| REACTOME ORA: terms tested / significant | 612 / 28 | PASS |
| REACTOME GSEA: terms tested / significant | 590 / 281 | PASS |
| Schema: required columns with nulls | 0 | PASS |
| ORA/GSEA column separation | odds_ratio null in GSEA; NES null in ORA | PASS |
| adj_pvalue < pvalue | 31 rows, all GSEA | PASS (expected -- GSEA uses permutation-based FDR, not BH; can legitimately be below nominal p) |
| GSEA NES direction | 383 positive NES (up in KO), 307 negative NES (down in KO) | PASS (biologically interpretable) |
| Protein-term mapping rows | 353,688 | PASS |
| in_significant_set / is_leading_edge populated | 27,276 / 88,281 rows True | PASS |
| All output files produced | 4 data files + 4 lollipop pairs + 20 running score PNGs | PASS |

**Biological note:** Top GSEA hits (mRNA processing, cytoplasmic translation, ribosome biogenesis, synaptic vesicle cycle) are enriched in the downregulated direction in KO. Top ORA hits (amino acid metabolism, mRNA processing, mitochondrion organization) overlap the same biology. Consistent with known SHANK3/synaptic KO phenotypes affecting translation and synaptic function.

## 8.3 Edge Case Test: Zero Significant Proteins (2026-04-07)

**Method:** CTXcyto DA results (8,403 proteins, 1 contrast) with `significant` column set to all `False`. This simulates a contrast with no significant proteins due to conservative thresholds or weak biological effect.

| Check | Result | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Script exits cleanly | Exit code 0, no exceptions | PASS |
| ORA skipped for both libraries | Warning logged: "no significant genes -- ORA skipped" (GO_BP, REACTOME) | PASS |
| GSEA runs normally | GO_BP: 2,934 tested / 409 significant; REACTOME: 590 tested / 281 significant | PASS |
| Enrichment results schema valid | 3,524 rows, 13 columns, all GSEA (0 ORA rows) | PASS |
| No ORA lollipop plots produced | Correct -- only GSEA lollipop + running score plots generated | PASS |
| Summary reports 0 significant genes | "Significant genes (ORA input): 0 / 8134", ORA sig terms = 0 for both libraries | PASS |
| Protein-term mapping valid | 263,941 rows, 8 columns; `in_significant_set` = False for all rows | PASS |
| GSEA results unaffected | GSEA results match normal run (same ranked list, same permutation results) | PASS |

**Conclusion:** The zero-significant-proteins edge case is handled correctly. ORA is skipped gracefully with a logged warning; GSEA proceeds normally because it uses the full ranked list. All output files are produced with valid schemas. No code changes required.

---

# 9. Change History

### 2026-03-23 -- Initial Stub

**Change:** Module documentation created from content previously in Section 3.3 of the monolithic project documentation.
**Reason:** Documentation restructured into master overview + module files.
**Affected processes:** All (initial design capture).
**Downstream effects:** None.

### 2026-03-30 -- Full Design Specification

**Change:** Complete rewrite of the module spec. Expanded from 111-line stub to full specification covering: single-process architecture, gene symbol preparation (unmapped handling, duplicate collapse), ORA via gseapy.enrich() with detected-proteins background, GSEA via gseapy.prerank() with t-statistic default ranking, local GMT files from MSigDB (GO_BP and KEGG defaults), gene set size filters (15-500), per-contrast independent enrichment, enrichment results table schema (12 columns), protein-term mapping table schema (8 columns), per-library FDR correction, lollipop plot visualization (custom Plotly, PNG + HTML), GSEA running score plots (gseapy built-in, PNG only), summary text file format, 10 design decisions, 10 parameters, testing plan.
**Reason:** Design session completed all architectural decisions for Module 05.
**Affected processes:** ENRICHMENT (new).
**Downstream effects:** Module 07 (Results Assembly) consumes the enrichment results table and protein-term mapping table for the SQLite database. The enrichment results schema (Section 4.7) and protein-term mapping schema (Section 4.8) define the data contracts.

### 2026-03-31 -- Implementation

**Change:** Module implemented and tested. Files created: `bin/enrichment.py`, `modules/local/enrichment/main.nf`. Wired into `workflows/prosift.nf` as the 8th multiMap branch (`enrich_params`). Enrichment params block added to `prosift_inputs/CTXcyto_WT_vs_CTXcyto_KO/CTXcyto_WT_vs_CTXcyto_KO_params.yml`.

**Design deviations from spec (accepted):**
- *Default libraries:* Spec listed GO_BP + KEGG as defaults. Available GMT files are GO_BP + Reactome (v2026.1). Reactome used as second default; KEGG can be added later.
- *`term_id` format:* Spec showed `GO:0006915`-style numeric IDs. MSigDB GMT files use `GOBP_APOPTOTIC_PROCESS`-style names. `term_id` and `term_name` both hold the MSigDB gene set name; no separate numeric GO ID is parsed.
- *GMT path resolution:* Paths in params.yml are resolved relative to the params.yml file's directory (not the working directory). This ensures relative paths work in both standalone testing and Nextflow execution.

**Key gseapy API notes (gseapy 1.1.12):**
- `gseapy.prerank()` result object: `res.res2d` (DataFrame), `res.results` (dict keyed by term name). Per-term data: `results[term]['hits']`, `results[term]['RES']`, `results[term]['lead_genes']`, `results[term]['nes']`, `results[term]['pval']`, `results[term]['fdr']`.
- GSEA FDR (`adj_pvalue` in the results table) is permutation-based, not BH. Can legitimately be less than the nominal p-value for some terms. This is expected behavior, not a bug.
- `gseapy.gseaplot()` signature: `gseaplot(term, hits, nes, pval, fdr, RES, rank_metric, ofname, ...)`.
- `threads=1` in `prerank()` for deterministic results; parallel threads can affect permutation outcomes.

**Test results:** CTXcyto standalone benchmark (2026-03-31). All spot-checks passed (see Section 8.2). Full stub-run test not yet done. [FOLLOW-UP NEEDED]

**Reason:** Phase 2 implementation milestone.
**Affected processes:** ENRICHMENT (new).
**Downstream effects:** Module 05 outputs now available for Module 06 (Database Queries) and Module 07 (Results Assembly) design.

### 2026-04-01 -- Spec Body Corrected to Match Implementation

**Change:** Updated spec body to resolve design deviations documented in the 2026-03-31 implementation entry. Three corrections: (1) Section 4.4 default libraries changed from GO_BP + KEGG to GO_BP + Reactome (KEGG moved to optional list). (2) Section 4.4 library short name example updated to include REACTOME. (3) Section 4.7 `term_id` and `term_name` descriptions corrected to reflect MSigDB GMT naming convention (`GOBP_APOPTOTIC_PROCESS` style, not `GO:0006915` numeric IDs); `library` example changed from `KEGG` to `REACTOME`.
**Reason:** Spec body was inconsistent with implementation; the change history documented the deviations but the spec text was never corrected.
**Affected processes:** None (documentation only).
**Downstream effects:** None.

### 2026-04-07 -- Zero-Significant-Proteins Edge Case Tested

**Change:** Acceptance criterion #10 (zero significant proteins) tested and confirmed passing. Test used CTXcyto DA results with `significant` column set to all `False`. ORA correctly skipped with logged warning for both libraries; GSEA ran normally using the full ranked list. All output files produced with valid schemas. Enrichment results table contained only GSEA rows (0 ORA). Protein-term mapping `in_significant_set` correctly `False` for all rows. Summary file correctly reported 0 significant genes. Section 8.3 added with full test results table; criterion #10 updated from `IMPLEMENTED` to `DONE`.
**Reason:** Criterion #10 was the only untested acceptance criterion. CTXcyto benchmark data always has significant proteins, so a synthetic input was needed.
**Affected processes:** None (testing and documentation only).
**Downstream effects:** None.

### 2026-04-07 -- Documentation Restructuring: Tiered Decision Register Intro

**Change:** Added introductory note to Section 5 (Design Decisions) explaining the tiered Decision Register approach: module-internal decisions in this spec, cross-cutting decisions in `proSIFT_project_history.Rmd`. All 10 existing Module 05 design decisions are module-internal (enrichment-specific implementation choices), so no cross-references were needed.
**Reason:** Completing the restructuring pattern established in Module 01 (2026-04-01) and already applied to Modules 02, 03a, 03b, and 04. Module 05 was the last module spec missing the introductory note.
**Affected sections:** 5 (Design Decisions introductory note added), 9 (this entry).
**Downstream effects:** None (documentation restructuring only). The restructuring pattern is now applied to all existing module specs (01-05) and will be used going forward for Modules 06-08.
